entry.title: Incorrect VAT reporting date transferred from SD to FI in Public Cloud
entry.content:
We are experiencing issues with the derivation of the Tax Reporting Date in accounting documents generated from SD billing documents in the Hungarian localization of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

According to the SAP documentation, the Tax Reporting Date and Tax Fulfilment Date can be derived via the configuration activity Define Derivation Rules for Business Transactions.

We found several scenarios in which the standard logic seems to produce incorrect dates.

1. Cancellation invoice

The derivation rule for normal invoices (OINV) is configured to use the fulfilment date for both tax dates. A normal invoice is created with a service rendered date of 30 June and cancelled on 7 July.

The generated FI document receives a Tax Reporting Date of 30 June, while according to Hungarian legislation we would expect the cancellation posting date (7 July).

Invoice date problem.docx

2. Collective invoice

For collective invoices, Hungarian regulations require both the Tax Reporting Date and the Tax Fulfilment Date to be derived from the latest fulfilment date among the referenced deliveries.

However, our debugging indicates that the fulfilment date is determined by class CL_FULFILLDATE_SERVICE, while the VAT date is determined by BAdI implementation CL_IM_IM_FULFILLMENT_ACC. In our tests, one date is taken from the first invoice item and the other from the last invoice item.

Invoice date problem.docx

3. Final invoice with down payment

For a final invoice containing a down-payment clearing item, the system seems to use the derivation rule maintained for OPERIO, although the business process is not a periodic settlement.

In our example, the final invoice receives:

  • Tax Reporting Date: 30.06.2026
  • Tax Fulfilment Date: 08.09.2026

while both dates should be 10.07.2026 according to our understanding of Hungarian VAT rules.

Invoice date problem.docx

Questions

  • Has anyone encountered similar behavior in the Hungarian localization of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  • Is this the intended standard behavior, or is it considered a defect?
  • Are there SAP Notes or recommended configuration settings that address these scenarios?
  • Is there any official guidance regarding the legal interpretation of these invoice types in Hungary?

entry.title: AI-Driven SAP Testing: The Next Evolution of SAP Quality Assurance
entry.content:
Technical Friction in Enterprise System Validation

Deploying updates across hybrid SAP landscapes introduces three distinct technical bottlenecks:

  • Complex Customization Maintenance: Custom enhancements (Z-tables, user exits, and custom ABAP developments) often break during SAP upgrades. Traditional automation scripts break with them because minor UI element ID changes or underlying schema updates render static object repositories obsolete.
  • Large-Scale Test Data Management: Validating end-to-end (E2E) transactions requires context-rich, compliant data across interconnected modules (e.g., Order-to-Cash, Procure-to-Pay). Manually provisioning this data creates severe testing bottlenecks.
  • Limited End-to-End Visibility: A standard SAP transaction rarely stays entirely inside SAP; it frequently triggers APIs, middleware (like SAP Integration Suite), and third-party legacy applications. Validating these distributed data flows requires specialized SAP integration testing services rather than simple UI automation.

Strategic Blueprint for AI-Powered Quality Assurance

Transforming an enterprise SAP QA function requires moving away from static test scripts and adopting an adaptive, three-tiered technical framework backed by robust SAP test strategy services:

1. Intelligent Process Discovery

Instead of relying on outdated documentation, AI mining tools analyze SAP transaction logs (ST03N data) and user execution paths. This extracts the actual, real-world business processes directly from the system, creating a highly accurate baseline for the test catalog.

2. Self-Healing Test Automation

When SAP updates modify field IDs, UI properties, or Fiori elements, traditional test scripts fail. SAP test automation services use computer vision and object-recognition algorithms to detect these UI changes in real time. The framework automatically updates the underlying object repository mid-execution, preventing false-positive test failures.

3. Predictive Test Prioritization (Change Impact Analysis)

AI engines parse SAP transport logs to map exactly which transport objects intersect with specific business processes. Instead of running a full suite for a minor hotfix, SAP regression testing services calculate a targeted execution path, running only the scripts directly exposed to the code change.

Performance Matrix: Traditional vs. Intelligent Testing

Validation MetricManual & Legacy AutomationAI-Driven SAP Testing
Defect DetectionLimited to predefined script assertions and manual observation.Proactively identifying edge-case defects via historical run logs and code analysis.
Script MaintenanceManual updates are required whenever a UI element or Fiori component shifts.Algorithmic self-healing resolves object-property changes on the fly.
Test Data PrepManual data staging; high risk of data aging and test degradation.Dynamic data masking, synthesis, and automated state injection.
Regression ScopeRigid, broad-spectrum execution; throttled by time and compute constraints.Dynamic execution scoped directly by change impact analysis.

Operational Advantages of Targeted Validation

Partnering with an experienced SAP testing services provider to deploy modern SAP automated testing tools yields definitive operational improvements:

Faster Release Cycles: By targeting only high-risk modules, regression testing timelines drop from weeks to hours, allowing organizations to keep pace with SAP’s continuous update cycles.

Lower Script Maintenance: Automated self-healing drops script maintenance queues by up to 70%, freeing SAP QA testing services teams from fixing broken selectors to focus on complex functional coverage.

Improved Functional Coverage: SAP functional testing expands past basic SAP screens to validate complex, multi-system workflows (e.g., confirming a Fiori app interaction successfully triggers an external warehouse API and downstream finance ledger entry).

Final Thought

The bottom line is simple. Enterprise SAP landscapes have grown too fast and too complex for teams to test everything manually or maintain fragile, old-school automation scripts. Each new update in the cloud or custom changes brings a risk of disruptive processes.

AI-driven testing solves this by acting as an intelligent filter. It figures out exactly what changed under the hood, heals automation scripts automatically when screens change, and runs only the specific tests that are actually at risk. By turning QA from a massive guessing game into a precise, automated operation, enterprises can safely deploy continuous software updates without risking system downtime or disrupting daily business operations.

entry.title: AI Agents vs. workflows - When to use what in SAP Sales and Service Cloud
entry.content:
Understanding when to use AI agents versus workflows in SAP Sales and Service Cloud is crucial for optimizing business processes. AI agents excel at autonomous decision-making and handling complex, unstructured tasks, while workflows provide structured, predictable automation for repetitive processes.

The choice depends on your specific business requirements, the complexity of tasks, and the desired level of human oversight.

What are AI Agents and workflows in SAP Sales and Service Cloud? 

Before we start comparing them, we need to understand what AI Agents and workflows are.

  • AI Agents in SAP Sales and Service Cloud are autonomous software entities that harness data from your SAP Customer Experience products to make decisions and drive business actions that make an impact.
  • Workflows in SAP Sales and Service Cloud are automated business process tools that help organizations streamline and standardize their operations by defining sequences of tasks, approvals, and actions that execute automatically when specific conditions are met.

Instead of viewing AI agents and workflows solely as competing technologies, organizations should evaluate them based on the business outcomes they aim to achieve. The following decision framework can help determine which approach is more suitable for a specific scenario.

Automation Level

One of the first questions organizations should ask is how much autonomy the process requires.

AI agents operate with a high degree of autonomy, using reasoning to dynamically adapt their actions to context and evolving inputs. Workflows, in contrast, follow predefined, rule-based steps that execute consistently but lack flexibility.

If the process requires stable, repeatable execution with limited variation, workflows are often the preferred choice. If the process depends on adaptability, contextual understanding, and real-time decision-making, AI agents provide greater value.

Decision Making

Another important consideration is how decisions are made within the process.

AI agents make decisions through independent analysis, leveraging context, and AI-driven reasoning to determine the best course of action. Workflows rely on predetermined logic and conditional branching defined upfront.

Organizations should therefore evaluate whether the process requires:

  • Predictable and fully controlled outcomes
  • Dynamic interpretation and contextual reasoning
  • The ability to adapt decisions over time

Workflows are easier to govern and validate, while AI agents are better suited for situations that require judgment, interpretation, and continuous learning.

Setup Complexity

The implementation approach also plays a key role in the decision process.

Implementing AI agents typically involves higher initial complexity, including configuration, training, fine-tuning, and integration with enterprise knowledge sources. Workflows are simpler to implement because they depend on clearly defined rules and process mapping.

Organizations looking for rapid deployment and operational stability may benefit from workflows first, while those investing in intelligent automation and long-term adaptability may prioritize AI agents despite the higher initial effort.

Data Processing

The nature of the data being processed is another critical decision factor.

AI agents can analyze unstructured data, identify patterns, and derive insights from sources such as documents, conversations, or free-text inputs. Workflows are optimized for processing structured data based on predefined rules and formats.

As part of the decision framework, organizations should ask:

  • Is the input structured or unstructured?
  • Does the process require contextual understanding?
  • Are decisions based on fixed logic or interpretation of information?

Processes driven primarily by structured transactional data are usually well-suited for workflows, while processes involving interpretation and analysis benefit more from AI agents.

Governance, Trust, and Human Oversight

Governance and trust should be considered before introducing AI-driven automation.

Before choosing an AI agent approach, organizations should evaluate:

  • What is the risk if the AI produces an incorrect result?
  • Where is human approval mandatory?
  • Should humans remain in the loop when the outcome is uncertain or not aligned with expectations?
  • What data and knowledge sources ground the AI-generated result?
  • How will outputs be monitored, validated, and audited?

Workflows naturally provide stronger predictability because all outcomes are predefined. AI agents introduce greater flexibility, but they also require governance frameworks, monitoring mechanisms, and clear escalation paths for exception handling.

For many organizations, the optimal approach is not full autonomy but controlled autonomy, where AI agents support decision-making while humans maintain oversight over high-impact actions.

Cost Considerations

Cost should be evaluated not only from an implementation perspective, but also from a long-term operational perspective.

AI agents generally require a higher upfront investment due to setup, training, integration, and governance requirements. However, they can significantly reduce manual work, improve operational efficiency, and adapt more effectively as business complexity grows.

Workflows typically involve lower implementation costs and faster time-to-value, making them attractive for stable and well-defined processes. However, as processes become more complex, maintaining large numbers of rules and exceptions may increase operational overhead.

Organizations should therefore balance:

  • Short-term implementation simplicity
  • Long-term scalability and adaptability
  • Operational efficiency gains
  • Maintenance effort over time

Defining Success Through KPIs

An effective decision framework should also define how success will be measured after implementation.

Regardless of whether organizations choose workflows, AI agents, or a hybrid approach, measurable KPIs are essential to evaluate business impact. Examples include:

  • First-time routing accuracy
  • Quote turnaround time
  • Reassignment rate
  • SLA breach reduction
  • Agent productivity and manual effort saved

These metrics help organizations determine whether automation is improving efficiency, reducing operational friction, and supporting business objectives.

 

Use case scenarios in SAP Sales and Service Cloud

Now, let's dive into concrete use case scenarios and answer the title question: AI Agents vs. workflows - When to use what in SAP Sales and Service Cloud?

On the Sales side, one of the use cases where an AI Agent is optimal is Quote generation and pricing. In this use case, sales reps often spend significant time extracting customer and product data from multiple sources before creating proposals. A Quote Creation Agent streamlines this by automatically detecting intent from incoming emails, extracting key information, and generating ready-to-send quotes while creating records in SAP Sales Cloud.

This reduces manual effort and shortens quote turnaround from days to hours, improving both speed and accuracy, though it is currently supported only in Sales Cloud V1. Meanwhile, in Sales Cloud V2, the solution for this use case is even more streamlined by using Business Information Extraction (BIE). This capability is used to configure extraction logic for AI-assisted creation of sales quotes from emails, allowing organizations to define the scenario that interprets the incoming request and supports quote creation. In the following blog post, more information can be found on How Business Information Extraction in SAP Sales Cloud V2 Accelerates Sales Response and Conversion.

Workflows, on the other hand, are ideal for more structured, rule-based scenarios such as the Opportunity approval process. When a deal exceeds a certain threshold, the workflow automatically triggers and routes the opportunity through sequential approval steps—from sales manager review to finance validation and executive sign-off—while sending notifications and managing escalation timers at each stage. The prerequisite for this workflow is clearly defined by the approval of hierarchies.

On the Service side, an AI agent can significantly improve efficiency by automatically analyzing unstructured customer communications and categorizing cases based on business requirements. Unlike static routing rules, AI agents can understand context and intent, ensuring complex enterprise cases reach the right technical expert on the first try.  With this Intelligent Case Classification & Routing, the AI Agent can drive up to 50% higher service staff productivity, with faster response times and a notable reduction in reassignment rates.

Workflows are optimal for Service Level Agreement (SLA) management because SLAs are usually crystal clear, with specific requirements for response times, resolution times, and escalation procedures. When a case is created, the SLA timer is triggered based on customer tier and case priority, and automated notifications are assigned to agents showing the remaining time. As thresholds approach, escalation workflows activate to ensure timely action, while management is notified in case of potential or actual breaches. For critical situations, the system can automatically adjust case priority and reallocate resources to maintain SLA compliance.

 

To conclude, AI agents are ideal for handling unstructured data and complex interactions, such as interpreting emails or managing dynamic customer conversations. Workflows excel at repetitive, structured scenarios such as approvals, task routing, and standardized processes.

What is the future of AI Agents & AI Assistants?

SAP is advancing its Autonomous CX vision — a core pillar of the Autonomous Enterprise — by deploying Joule Assistants and Joule Agents that orchestrate multi-step workflows across the full customer lifecycle, from first engagement through post-sales activities.  These agents go beyond simple automation, reasoning and acting directly in core processes, turning complexity into clarity.

Grounded in live business data, end-to-end processes, and SAP business semantics, Joule Assistants coordinate specialized agents across CX and the wider enterprise to deliver connected, context-aware, and trusted outcomes. Joule Studio further enables organizations to build, orchestrate, deploy, and govern custom agents and workflows securely at enterprise scale—accelerating the transition from isolated AI pilots to production-ready autonomy.

See below the roadmap for further consideration:

 

Importance of understanding the strong suits of AI Agents and workflows

Understanding the strengths of AI agents and workflows in SAP Sales and Service Cloud is essential for designing efficient and scalable business processes. Each serves a distinct purpose—AI agents bring intelligence and adaptability, while workflows provide structure and control. Knowing when to use each ensures that organizations avoid overcomplicating simple processes or underutilizing automation in complex scenarios. 

 

Why is a hybrid approach recommended? 

We recommend that you explore your options and avoid using only one of the options mentioned in this article.  AI agents and workflows address fundamentally different types of business problems, and relying on a single approach limits overall efficiency. A hybrid approach represents the most pragmatic path forward, enabling organizations to harness the power of both technologies while mitigating the risks of either pure AI autonomy or rigid workflow automation.

entry.title: Which WalkMe Certification exam do I need? C_WME_2506 or C_WME_2601?
entry.content:
Hello WalkMeTeers, I am confused and I hope you can help me. I am a Partner, already SAP-certified as a WalkMe DAP Consultant and also in Positioning/Selling WalkMe DAP. I have been getting email reminders to take my C_WME WalkMe Digital Adoption Consultant exam to keep my certification. I re-prepared for that (I thought) by going through the current learning.sap.com Learning Journey for WalkMe Digital Adoption Platform (WME001, WME002 and WME003), which is roughly equivalent to the training.walkme.com Fundamentals, Builder 1 and Builder 2 courses that I took in 2025 in training.walkme.com. After I took the training.walkme.com courses in 2025, I passed the old-style SAP proctored exams for WalkMe DAP Consultant (2 hours, 60 questions) and Positioning/Selling WalkMe (1 hour, 30 questions) in 2025 to get my SAP certifications in those. The WalkMe DAP Consultant recertification is the one I'm working on now. 

When I launched the link from the email reminder to take my WalkMe DAP Consultant recertification exam today, it took me to C_WME_2506, which turned out to be the old-style Learning Hub multiple-choice format exam, 8 questions, untimed, take it as many times as you want. Here's the problem - the C_WME_2506 exam questions were all about Business Transformation Platform, SAP Asset Lifecycle Management, SAP Signavio, and SAP Lean IX. I don't remember taking any courses about those. I passed anyway on the second try, but it seems like it's the wrong exam because it asked nothing about WalkMe.  

So I have three questions:

  1. Am I still certified as an SAP WalkMe DAP Consultant because I passed C_WME_2506, even though the questions weren't about WalkMe?
  2. Am I required to take the new AI-powered scenario based exam C_WME_2601, if I am already certified under C_WME_2506 until July 2027? I don't want to burn one of my 4 allowed attempts on C_WME_2601 if I'm not required to take it.
  3. Am I now certified as a BTP consultant because I passed C_WME_2506, since the questions were about BTP and not WalkMe?

Thanks in advance for any assistance/advice you can provide.

entry.title: How to Enable Checkboxes for Configuration Parameters in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction:

In this blog, I will first demonstrate how to control payload logging using an externalized parameter with true/false values. Then, I will replace the text-based parameter with a checkbox and show how to achieve the same functionality in a more intuitive and user-friendly way.

 

Part 1: Using a Text-Based Externalized Parameter to Control Payload Logging

In the example below, I have added a Content Modifier to declare the Exchange Property enableLog, which will be used to control payload logging. 

The enableLog parameter has been externalized with a default value of true.

Next, I use the enableLog parameter in the Groovy script to determine whether the payload should be logged. Based on the configured value (true or false), the script either logs the payload or skips the logging step.

Important: Although the externalized parameter is configured with the values true or false, SAP Cloud Integration passes the value to the Groovy script as a String, not as a Boolean. Therefore, you should explicitly compare the value of enableLog with the string "true" (or "false"). This comparison returns a Boolean result, which can then be used directly in the if condition to control the logging logic.

I configured the externalized parameter enableLog with the value true. Since the condition evaluated to true, the Groovy script logged the payload successfully, as shown in the example below.

Part 2: Using a Checkbox-Based Externalized Parameter to Control Payload Logging

To follow along with this example, download the artifacts and extract the ZIP file. After extracting it, navigate to the following directory:

CheckBox\src\main\resources

This folder contains the files that need to be modified to enable checkbox-based externalized parameters.

Modify the parameter type from string to boolean, as shown below. This change instructs SAP Cloud Integration to render the externalized parameter as a checkbox instead of a text field.

Zip the file as below:

Upload the Zip file as below: 

Now you will get the checkbox as below.

Conclusion: 

In this blog, we explored two approaches for controlling payload logging in SAP Cloud Integration using externalized parameters. We first implemented the traditional approach with a text-based parameter that accepts true or false values. We then enhanced the solution by changing the parameter type from String to Boolean, which automatically renders it as a checkbox in the configuration.

Using a checkbox provides a cleaner and more intuitive user experience, eliminating the need to manually enter true or false values and reducing the risk of configuration errors. This simple enhancement can be applied to any Boolean configuration parameter in your integration flows, making them easier to configure and maintain.

entry.title: Program activation via Eclipse seems okay but is not (constant overflow>internal error)
entry.content:
If I activate a program with a very specific error like the code below, if I don't pay attention to the very bottom status bar "Activation failed" (because I've got a very large screen; NB: the screen size has been adapted for the screenshot), the two other error indicators don't show any error (no gray led on the P icon, no error in the problems view) (*):

If I activate again, I can see the syntax error message in the problems log :

(*) There are 3 error indicators: the gray led on the "object type icon" (the "P" brown discus here), the presence of errors in the problems view, the message "Activation failed" in the bottom status bar. These 3 indicators are usually synchronized, either error or not error; here, one syntax error:

For information, the backend editor (SE80/SE38/SE24) better handles the situation by displaying the exact error message, although the source code status becomes active as is the case for Eclipse:

Is there anything that can be done to avoid this issue? Is it fixed or improved in more recent Eclipse/backend versions?

Thanks.

Sandra

Reproduced on ABAP 7.58 SP 1 ON-PREMISE and Eclipse 2025-09

NB, here's the code as text:

REPORT zdemo.
*a = 1.
CONSTANTS c_xx TYPE bstmg VALUE '99999999999.99'.

entry.title: Re: Program activation via Eclipse seems okay but is not (constant overflow>internal error)
entry.content:
The reason is internal to the SAP compiler: the overflow for constants is not checked during the syntax check (i.e. the ABAP statement "SYNTAX-CHECK FOR abap_source_table"), it's why the program status becomes active, but the overflow is checked only during the generation of the ABAP Load (i.e. the ABAP statement "GENERATE REPORT program MESSAGE-ID generation_error_message").

The issue seems to be in Eclipse ADT, because the ABAP backend returns the generation error:

entry.title: Re: Issues in delta load in SAP Datasphere from S4HANA CD...
entry.content:
Look ay this blog: CDS based data extraction - Part II Delta Handling 

For extracting data from tables in S/4HANA where the standard CDS view is not delta-enabled, the recommended approach is as follows:

  • Identify or create a delta-enabled CDS view that exposes the required data. This may involve redesigning the source data model or developing a custom CDS view with appropriate delta extraction annotations and ensuring delta support on base tables.
  • If delta functionality is mandatory and cannot be achieved with the standard views, consider implementing a custom data extraction approach. This could include using timestamp-based generic delta mechanisms or setting up Change Data Capture (CDC) where feasible.
  • Avoid utilizing CDS views that contain complex definitions or AMDPs, as these do not support delta extraction in replication flows.

Regarding limitations in SAP Datasphere replication flows for initial and delta replication:

  • Initial and delta replication flows have stricter limits on parallel jobs and the number of objects due to the overhead of delta tracking.
  • Views with complex definitions or multiple delta mechanisms are not supported for delta replication.
  • Customer extensions on CDS views may result in replication failures.
  • CDS views with AMDP or complex associations are only supported for initial loads; delta replication is not supported.
  • If delta extraction is not supported, only initial load replication will be feasible.

For proper delta extraction configuration, the following annotations must be included in the CDS view:

  • `@Analytics.dataExtraction.changeDataCapture: true`
  • `@Analytics.dataExtraction.enabled: true`
  • Appropriate delta key fields or timestamp fields for delta identification.

entry.title: Re: Asset Useful Life Extension – Depreciation Recalculat...
entry.content:
In summary:

  • Create a new Valuation Interval with an effective start date of 30.09.2026.
  • Set the useful life to 60 years within this new interval.
  • The system will then recalculate depreciation prospectively from that date.
  • Past posted depreciation (prior to and including 30.09.2026) will be preserved and will not be retroactively altered.

This approach aligns with SAP's standard behavior for depreciation recalculation and valuation intervals, specifically designed for scenario changes effective from future dates within closed accounting periods

Some references: 

  • 3392995 - Depreciation value is unexpected after change the useful life in Manage Fixed Assets app - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • 2813681 - Change of Useful Life Retriggers Depreciation Values for Previous Period

entry.title: From Code to Strategy: How MCP, CPI, and Lightweight Agents Unlock AI‑Driven SAP Integrations
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) allows enterprises to connect diverse systems through iFlows — integration flows that define how data moves and transforms between endpoints. By combining CPI with the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and a lightweight Agent, we can expose SAP processes as consumable services — enabling seamless orchestration, automation, and intelligent interaction across enterprise systems.

 

Understanding the Components

SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)

SAP CPI is the integration backbone of the SAP ecosystem. It enables secure, scalable, and real-time data exchange between SAP and non-SAP systems. In this architecture, CPI acts as the execution layer, where business logic resides. The iFlow processes requests coming from the MCP Server, performs necessary transformations, and interacts with SAP backend systems such as S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, or Finance modules.

In the AI world: CPI provides the structured data foundation that intelligent agents rely on. By exposing iFlows as APIs, it allows AI systems to access enterprise data securely and contextually — bridging traditional ERP with modern AI-driven automation.

 

Model Context Protocol (MCP) Server

The MCP Server is the middleware bridge between the Agent and SAP CPI. It defines “tools” — essentially API endpoints — that the Agent can call to trigger specific SAP processes. It handles authentication, routing, and error management, ensuring that requests from the Agent are properly formatted and securely transmitted to CPI.

In the AI world: MCP acts as the contextual translator between natural language and enterprise APIs. It enables AI agents to understand what business functions are available and how to invoke them, making enterprise systems conversational and intelligent.

 

Copilot Agent

The Agent is the frontline interface — a lightweight application (built with FastAPI or similar frameworks) that interacts with users or other systems. It consumes the MCP tools and provides a simple way to trigger SAP processes, either through a web UI or API calls.

In the AI world: The Agent represents the intelligence layer — capable of interpreting user intent, orchestrating workflows, and connecting to enterprise data through MCP and CPI. It’s the component that transforms static APIs into dynamic, AI-driven interactions.

 

Key Steps

1. Expose the iFlow in SAP CPI

  • Design and deploy your iFlow (e.g., invoice retrieval or employee data).

  • Ensure it’s accessible via HTTPS with proper authentication.

2. Create the MCP Server

  • Define tools mapping to CPI endpoints.

  • Configure the MCP server to expose these tools securely.

  • Example endpoint: http://localhost:8000/tools/sayHello

3. Consume with an Agent

  • Build a lightweight Agent using FastAPI.

  • Connect it to the MCP server and expose an endpoint like http://localhost:9000/agent/sayHello.

  • Provide a simple UI for users to trigger CPI processes.

 

Tools Used

  • SAP CPI → iFlow design and deployment

  • MCP Server → Tool definition and orchestration

  • FastAPI → Agent development

  • OAuth2 / SAP IAS → Authentication and security

 

Architecture Overview

Embed your architecture diagram here showing the flow: User → Copilot Agent → MCP Server → SAP CPI → SAP Backend Systems

Figure 1: End-to-end architecture connecting the Agent, MCP Server, and SAP CPI iFlow.

Step: Create a Simple iFlow in SAP CPI

Design a basic iFlow in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) that accepts a name as input payload and returns a greeting message. For example, if the payload contains "Dibyajyoti", the iFlow should respond with "Hello Dibyajyoti".

Once the development is complete, deploy the iFlow to generate the endpoint URL. This URL will be used later by the MCP Server and Copilot Agent to invoke the iFlow and integrate it into the overall architecture.

"https://<SUBACCOUNT>.it-cpitrial06-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http/hello"

Step 2: Test the iFlow Endpoint in Postman

Once the iFlow is deployed in SAP CPI, you’ll receive an endpoint URL. To validate it, use Postman:

  1. Open Postman and create a new request.

  2. Set the method to POST (or GET, depending on your iFlow design).

  3. Enter the endpoint URL generated during deployment (e.g., https://cpi.example.com/http/endpoint/sayHello).

  4. Authentication:

    • Username → Client ID

    • Password → Client Secret These credentials are provided when you configure the CPI tenant and are required for secure access.

  5. Payload:

    • In the request body, pass the input JSON or text payload (e.g., { "name": "Dibyajyoti" }).

  6. Send the request and verify the response.

    • Expected output: Hello Dibyajyoti

 

Step 3: Set Up the MCP Environment

Before working with the MCP Server, ensure your development environment is ready. You’ll need the following installed on your system:

  • Python (version 3.9 or above recommended)

  • PIP (Python package manager)

  • Visual Studio Code (for editing and running your project)


Step 4: Create the Project Folder Structure

To organize your MCP setup, create the following folders:

  1. Main Project Folder

    • Name: MCPPROJECT

  2. Subfolders inside MCPPROJECT

    • SAP_MCP_SERVER → Contains the MCP Server files (server.py, .env, requirements.txt)

    • COPILOT_AGENT → Contains the Agent files (agent.py, templates/index.html, requirements.txt)

 

SAP_MCP_SERVER

.env---->

requirements.txt---->

server.py---->

COPILOT_AGENT

requirements.txt------>

agent.py------->

create a subfolder

index.html--------->

 

Step 5: Run the MCP Server and Copilot Agent

Inside sap_mcp_server

Install dependencies and start the MCP Server:

pip install -r requirements.txt
uvicorn server:app --reload --port 8000

 

  • pip install -r requirements.txt → Installs all required Python packages.

  • uvicorn server:app --reload --port 8000 → Launches the MCP Server on port 8000 with auto-reload enabled.

Inside copilot_agent

Install dependencies and start the Copilot Agent:

pip install -r requirements.txt
uvicorn agent:app --reload --port 9000
​

 

  • pip install -r requirements.txt → Installs Agent dependencies.

  • uvicorn agent:app --reload --port 9000 → Launches the Agent on port 9000 with auto-reload enabled.

Test the Agent

Once both services are running, open the Agent URL in your browser:

http://localhost:9000/

 This will load the Agent’s web interface, allowing you to trigger the MCP tools and ultimately invoke the SAP CPI iFlow.

 

Conclusion

By exposing SAP CPI iFlows through an MCP Server and consuming them with an Agent, enterprises can create a modular, API-driven integration layer. This approach simplifies access to SAP processes, improves reusability, and lays the foundation for AI-powered enterprise automation — where intelligent agents can interact with SAP systems naturally, securely, and efficiently.

Regards,

Dibyajyoti Nanda

https://www.linkedin.com/in/dibyajyoti-nanda-23907429/

entry.title: BKP - Flexible Plant Determination Through BadI for Customer Returns
entry.content:
Returns processing is one of the more complex areas of order management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. A question that comes up frequently when setting up returns is: how does the system decide which plant should receive the returned goods?In many real-world scenarios, such as large enterprises with complex organizational structures spanning multiple company codes and, as a result, many plants, the answer depends on a combination of business rules — rules that are too specific to handle through standard master data or configuration alone.

This blog explains how to use the Flexible Plant Determination feature to implement custom logic for plant determination in returns order items.

Flexible Plant Determination

Flexible plant determination lets you implement custom logic to determine the receiving plant for returns order items (document type CBAR), instead of relying solely on plant determination from master data, configuration, or reference documents.

The custom logic is implemented by using the Business Add-In (BAdI):

SD_SLS_DETERMINE_RETURNS_PLANT — Determination of Plants in Returns Order Items

This BAdI is called when a returns order is created or changed. It gives you access to a rich set of document attributes, such as sales area, sold-to party, return reason, business partner data, and even custom fields — which you can evaluate to determine the appropriate plant. This makes it possible to satisfy complex business requirements that cannot be expressed through standard configuration.

Plant Determination Priority

Before diving into the implementation, it is important to understand the overall plant determination sequence and when custom logic comes to play.

As a general rule, when creating or changing a returns order, the system determines the plant in the following order:

1Manual EntryIf you enter a plant directly in the item, this value takes precedence over all other determination logic.
2Custom Logic (by BAdI)

If no manual entry is provided, the system calls the BAdI implementation (if one exists). If the BAdI returns a plant, this value is used.

3Reference DocumentIf no custom logic applies and a reference document exists, the system copies the plant from the reference document.
4Standard DeterminationIf none of the above applies, the system determines the plant using master data and configuration.

However, depending on different channels (different apps or APIs) you use to process customer returns, there are exceptions to the priority order. For details, see the following graphic:

Prerequisites for Using BAdI Logic

Before you can use the BAdI, you must activate the flexible plant determination feature in the configuration activity:

Configure Returns Order Type for Standard Return (ID: 106396)

If this feature is not activated, the system will not call your BAdI implementation at all — custom logic will simply be ignored.

Note: Activating this feature also causes the system to redetermine the plant when ship-to party data or return reason changes on the document. This happens regardless of whether you have a BAdI implementation in place. But if you use the classic UI for returns order processing, such as the “Create Customer Returns Without Reference” app, the plant redetermination is automatically supported without activation.

Fields Available for Custom Logic

The BAdI provides access to header, item, and partner data of the returns order. The custom fields created for the document header and items are also available in BAdI implementation.

You can use these fields as input for your logic:

Returns Order Header DataReturns Order Item DataHeader Partner DataItem Partner Data
  • Returns order number
  • Sales organization
  • Distribution channel
  • Division
  • Sold-to party
  • Order reason
  • Reference document
  • Reference document category
  • Customer reference
  • <Any custom fields>
Note: Header data change does not trigger plant redetermination. See Important Notes at the end of the blog for details.
  • Returns order number
  • Returns order item number
  • Product
  • Return reason
  • Reference document
  • Reference document item
  • Reference document category
  • <Any custom fields>
  • Partner function
  • Customer
  • Supplier
  • Personnel
  • Contact person
  • Country
  • Region
  • Postal code
  • Address ID
  • Returns order item number
  • Partner function
  • Customer
  • Supplier
  • Personnel
  • Contact person
  • Country
  • Region
  • Postal code
  • Address ID

 For more information on custom fields, see help documents for respective apps or APIs extensibility.

Implementing Custom Plant Determination Logic

Approach 1: Direct Logic in the BAdI

For straightforward cases, you can implement the determination rules directly inside the BAdI method. To do so, use the Custom Logic app as an extensibility specialist (business role template: SAP_BR_EXTENSIBILITY_SPEC). In the Custom Logic app, search for SD_SLS_DETERMINE_RETURNS_PLANT and create an implementation with your custom code.

Example:

IF     customerreturn-SDDocumentReason  = '009'
   AND customerreturn-SalesOrganization = '1010'.
    LOOP AT customerreturnitem_partners INTO item_partner
      WHERE SalesDocumentItem = customerreturnitem-SalesDocumentItem
        AND PartnerFunction   = 'WE'.
    ENDLOOP.
    IF item_partner-country = 'DE'
   AND item_partner-region  = 'BE'.
        plant = '1020'.
    RETURN.
    ENDIF.
ENDIF.

IF customerreturnitem-returnreason = '001'.
    plant = '1030'.
ENDIF.

In this example, the system determines the plant using this rule: If the order reason is 009 and the sales organization is 1010, the logic checks the ship-to party (partner function WE) on the item. If that partner is located in Germany (DE) and the Berlin region (BE), the system assigns plant 1020.

If the system can't find a plant using the first rule, then it continues to find using the second rule: If the return reason on the item is 001, the system assigns plant 1030.

This approach is easy to set up and works well when the number of rules is small and changes are infrequent. When the rule set grows — for example, when multiple sales organizations, regions, and return reasons each map to different plants — consider moving to a decision table as described in Approach 2.

Approach 2: Using a Decision Table

For more complex scenarios — where the determination depends on multiple conditions and combinations of field values — maintaining logic directly in code can quickly become difficult to read and maintain. Every new rule requires a code change, a transport, and a test cycle.

When to consider a decision table:

  • You have more than a handful of determination rules.
  • The rules change frequently and should be maintainable without developer involvement.
  • Multiple field combinations map to different plants and the combinations are expected to grow over time.
  • You want to document determination logic in a structured, auditable format.

In such cases, you can externalize the mapping using a decision table:

  1. Create a custom business object (for example, a Z-table) to store combinations of field values (such as sales organization, return reason, sold-to party region) alongside their corresponding plant values.
  2. At runtime, read this object from within the BAdI implementation and match the incoming document data against the stored rules to determine the plant.

This approach decouples the determination rules from the BAdI code. Business users or configurators can update the table without touching ABAP, which significantly reduces the cost of ongoing maintenance.

For more information, see Creating Custom Business Objects.

Step-by-Step Example: Using a Decision Table

Step 1: Open the Custom Business Objects app as extensibility specialist.

Step 2: Create a new business object with the required information and settings.

Check data is correct on the Nodes tab and change it if necessary:

Step 3: On the Fields tab, add the fields for your decision table and publish the business object.

Step 4: Once publishing completes successfully, use the UI link to access the decision table interface.

Step 5: In the decision table, maintain all the value mappings you need to determine plants.

Step 6: Implement custom code in the BAdI to read the business object data and determine the plant with matching data.

LOOP AT customerreturnitem_partners INTO item_partner
  WHERE SalesDocumentItem = customerreturnitem-SalesDocumentItem
    AND PartnerFunction   = 'WE'.
ENDLOOP.
SELECT Plant
  FROM YY1_PLANTDECISIONTABLE WITH PRIVILEGED ACCESS
  WHERE SalesOrganization = @customerreturn-salesorganization
    AND SDDocumentReason  = @customerreturn-SDDocumentReason
    AND Division          = @customerreturn-organizationdivision
    AND Country           = @item_partner-country
    AND PostalCode        = @item_partner-postalcode
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_plant)
  UP TO 1 ROWS.
IF lt_plant IS NOT INITIAL.
  plant = lt_plant[ 1 ]-plant.
ENDIF.

Important Notes

·      Header Data Changes Do Not Trigger Redetermination:

Once a plant has been determined by custom logic, subsequent changes to header data do not re-trigger plant determination for that item — even if the updated data would match a different rule. If you need the plant to be redetermined when header data changes, implement the additional BAdI:

SD_SLS_REPROCESS_ITEMS_HEAD — Reprocessing of Sales Document Items Based on Header Data Changes

·      Order Reason Behavior When Creating Reference via API:

When creating a returns order with reference using APIs, be aware of the following: if your custom logic uses the order reason as a condition, the system will not apply that logic to determine the plant. This is because the system reads the order reason from the reference document, not from the payload you provide. As a result, your custom logic cannot match on the order reason, and the system copies the plant from the reference document instead.

Workaround: Specify a return reason at the item level in your API payload and your BAdI logic will be applied.

Summary

Flexible plant determination gives you a clean, supported extension point for implementing business-specific returns routing logic in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The BAdI SD_SLS_DETERMINE_RETURNS_PLANT provides the flexibility to meet your business requirements. Here is a quick recap of the key points:

  • Activation: Must be enabled in Configure Order Type for Standard Return
  • Priority: Custom logic runs after manual entry but before reference document and standard determination
  • Simple rules: Implement directly in the BAdI method
  • Complex rules: Externalize mappings to a decision table (custom business object) for easier maintenance

For more information, see the SAP Help documentation: Flexible Plant Determination for Returns Order Items.

entry.title: Learning Journey: Applying SAP Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
There's a learning journey on SAP Learning that I want to highlight: Applying SAP Digital Manufacturing.

If you're working with SAP Digital Manufacturing as a consultant or business user, this is a structured path through everything you need to know to position, implement, and operate the solution.

It includes 11 intermediate level courses, and leads to the Achievement Badge below:

Learning Scope

  • Understanding how to position SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • Getting an overview about SAP Digital Manufacturing and SAP Digital Manufacturing for Insights
  • Understanding how to collect manufacturing data and how to create a data collection concept
  • Configuring master data for discrete-, and process manufacturing
  • Configuring and analyzing master data for discrete-, and process  manufacturing
  • Understanding the execution of shopfloor control processes in the discrete-, and process manufacturing area using SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • Exploring the extensibility mechanisms in SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • understanding and configuring the shop floor integration with SAP Digital Manufacturing to enable machine connectivity
  • Understand how to set up and connect the on-premise applications SAP Production Connector and SAP Cloud Connector required for machine connectivity with the cloud application SAP Digital Manufacturing

What it covers

The learning journey walks you through the full scope of SAP DM, from the ground up:

  • Positioning and overview — You can build a solid understanding of the SAP Digital Manufacturing platform and its Insights component, so you know how it fits into a broader manufacturing landscape.

  • Data collection strategy — You can learn how manufacturing data collection works and how to approach strategy development for your specific environment.

  • Master data configuration — You can configure master data for both discrete and process manufacturing, two distinct manufacturing models with different requirements.

  • Shopfloor execution — You can execute shopfloor control processes end-to-end, which is where most of the day-to-day manufacturing work happens.

  • Extensibility — You can explore the extensibility mechanisms available within SAP DM to adapt the platform to specific business needs.

  • Machine connectivity — You can set up shop floor integration including machine connectivity, using SAP Production Connector and SAP Cloud Connector to bridge on-premise systems with the cloud.

Who it's for

The learning journey is designed for consultants and business users working with SAP Digital Manufacturing. The only prerequisite is basic knowledge of planning and manufacturing, so you don't need to be a deep technical expert to get started.

Why it matters

SAP Digital Manufacturing covers a wide surface area: from how production orders flow through the shop floor, to how machines report data, to how you configure the system for a process plant versus a discrete factory. Having a structured learning path that ties all of that together in one place saves a lot of time compared to piecing it together from documentation and trial and error.

If you're new to SAP DM or want to fill gaps in your knowledge, this is a good place to start.

Explore the learning journey here: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/applying-sap-digital-manufacturing

Stay tuned for more! Thanks,
Manoel Costa
